sections.heading: Clinical Note
sections.section_value:
Chief Complaint
Evaluation of abnormal renal ultrasound with an atrophic right kidney.
History of Present Illness
Ashley James presents with an atrophic right kidney, discovered incidentally on a renal ultrasound. The patient reports experiencing abdominal pain and right-sided pain approximately one week prior to presentation, described as a severe, localized pain that prompted her to bend over in distress. The initial concern was appendicitis, and she was treated with intravenous fluids and a course of antibiotics. However, the pain persisted and worsened the following day, prompting her parents to seek further evaluation. Subsequently, she underwent an ultrasound, revealing a small kidney on one side. She has been experiencing intermittent episodes of nausea and vomiting, and reports burning sensations when urinating, particularly after consuming certain beverages like soda. She has a family history of kidney disease, with her father having undergone a kidney transplant and her great aunt also experiencing kidney issues. Her grandfather had posterior urethral strictures and obstructive neuropathy, leading to dialysis dependence and ultimately, death at age 47. Ashley reports no prior history of significant medical issues prior to the discovery of the kidney abnormality.
Findings

sections.heading: Clinical Note
sections.section_value:
Physical exam reveals a well-appearing 14-year-old female, alert and oriented, with no acute distress. Vital signs are within normal limits. Lung auscultation is clear bilaterally. Heart sounds are regular. Palpation reveals no organomegaly or tenderness. Laboratory results are pending, including a complete blood count, comprehensive metabolic panel, and urinalysis. Renal ultrasound demonstrates a significantly atrophied right kidney and a larger left kidney, with no evidence of obstruction or mass.
Assessment
Atrophic right kidney; Clinical suspicion of chronic kidney disease, stage 2. Family history of renal disease.
Plan
Order comprehensive metabolic panel, complete blood count, and urinalysis. Schedule follow-up renal ultrasound in three months to monitor kidney size and function. Recommend a referral to a pediatric nephrologist for further evaluation and management. Counsel the patient and her parents on the importance of hydration, avoiding nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs, and monitoring urine output. Advise avoidance of carbonated beverages due to potential exacerbation of urinary symptoms. Schedule a follow-up appointment in four weeks to assess symptom progression and review laboratory results.
